sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=25, col=13).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 71, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: VERIFIED(step 2-7): Clicking red block (4x3, ~40px) rotates perimeter blocks clockwise by 1 position. Clicking green block or colored blocks does nothing. Green strip at col 0 shrinks 5px per rotation.
- Object taxonomy: Red block (40px, left edge)=rotation trigger. Light green strip (col 0)=progress bar. Light green block (40px, right edge)=fixed decoration. Colored 2x2/4x4 blocks rotate on perimeter. Yellow 2x2 markers=fixed indicators. Black 2x2 blocks=HUD.
- HUD map: Left green strip (col 0) shrinks 5px per rotation from 64px initial. Top gray area has black blocks. Charcoal background.
- Win pattern: Rotate until green progress bar depletes. Level completes when strip reaches 0.
- Level log: Level 1: Click red block repeatedly (8 rotations) until green strip at col 0 shrinks from 64px to 0. Score=1.
- Cross-level notes: Levels have different layouts but same rotation mechanic via red block trigger.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 63 actions; a strong score needs about 38 or fewer. You are 1.7x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Both (17,20) and (25,13) rotate blocks in the same direction (blocks shifted similarly). The strip went from 64->63->62. So both triggers do the same thing - rotate blocks clockwise and shrink the strip by 1px.
The key question remains: what is the completion condition? The strip never reaches 0 before the cycle completes (61 rotations), so it's not about strip depletion.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the completion condition is about the colored blocks forming a specific pattern. Looking at the current state, I see blocks arranged in what looks like rows/columns. Maybe the goal is to rotate until all blocks are aligned in a specific way.
Looking at the current image, I notice there are colored blocks in what appears to be a cross-like pattern with yellow markers at the center. The blocks seem to be rotating around these yellow markers.
Wait, let me reconsider. Maybe the level has TWO independent rotation systems - one for each red block - and I need to rotate them independently to reach a target configuration. But from my test, both (17,20) and (25,13) seem to rotate the same blocks in the same direction.
Let me look more carefully at the block positions. Before clicking (25,13):
- w at (26, 17), b at (26, 20), w at (26, 26), S at (26, 29), p at (26, 32), w at (26, 35), S at (26, 38)
After clicking (25,13):
- b at (26, 17), w at (26, 23), S at (26, 26), p at (26, 29), w at (26, 32), S at (26, 35), w at (26, 44)
The blocks shifted: w->b at col 17, b->w at col 20->23, etc. This looks like a cyclic shift.
Let me think about the "about 38" target. If the cycle is 61, and I need to reach a specific configuration, maybe the target is at rotation 23 (since 61-23=38). But I already tried 38 rotations and it didn't work...
Wait, maybe the issue is that I need to click a SPECIFIC red block, not just any red block. Let me look at the layout again:
- Red block at (16-19, 19-21) - this is the "top-middle" one I clicked at (17, 20)
- Red block at (25-28, 13-15) - this is the "middle-left" one I clicked at (25, 13)
- Red block at (34-37, 13-15) - this is the "bottom-left" one I haven't clicked yet
Maybe each red block controls a different set of rotating blocks! Let me check if clicking (34, 13) affects different blocks.
Actually, looking at the block positions more carefully, I notice the blocks are arranged in what looks like three horizontal "rings" or rows:
1. Top row around row 17
2. Middle row around row 26
3. Bottom row around row 35
And the red blocks are positioned:
- (17, 20) - near the top row
- (25, 13) - near the middle row
- (34, 13) - near the bottom row
Maybe each red block rotates a different ring! Let me test by clicking (34, 13) and seeing which blocks move.
But wait, I already clicked (17, 20) and (25, 13), and both seemed to rotate ALL blocks. Let me look more carefully at which blocks actually moved.
Before (17,20) click: I don't have the exact initial state, but from the output after (17,20):
- b at (17, 23), p at (17, 26), Y at (17, 29), p at (17, 32), b at (17, 35)
- S at (20, 23), S at (20, 35)
- p at (23, 23)
- w at (26, 17), b at (26, 20), w at (26, 26), S at (26, 29), p at (26, 32), w at (26, 35), S at (26, 38)
- etc.
After (25,13) click:
- b at (17, 23), p at (17, 26), Y at (17, 29), p at (17, 32), b at (17, 35) - SAME
- S at (20, 23), S at (20, 35) - SAME
- p at (23, 23) - SAME
- b at (26, 17), w at (26, 23), S at (26, 26), p at (26, 29), w at (26, 32), S at (26, 35), w at (26, 44) - CHANGED
So the top row (row 17) didn't change after clicking (25, 13)! Only the middle row (row 26) changed. This suggests that (25, 13) only rotates the middle ring, not all blocks!
Similarly, (17, 20) might only rotate the top ring. Let me check by clicking (17, 20) again and seeing if only the top row changes.
This is a key insight! Each red block controls a different ring of blocks. The completion condition might be to align all three rings to a target configuration.
Let me now test clicking (34, 13) to see if it controls the bottom ring.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Key insight: (25,13) only changed row 26 blocks, not row 17 blocks!
# Each red block likely controls a different ring.
# Let me verify by clicking (34,13) and checking which blocks move.

# Current state after (17,20) then (25,13)
seg = current_frame.segmentation
small_blocks = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['pixels'] == 4 and n['color'] not in ('N', 'B', 'g', 'G')]
positions_before = {}
for n in small_blocks:
    b = n['boundary']
    center = (sum(p[0] for p in b)//4, sum(p[1] for p in b)//4)
    positions_before[n['hash'][:8]] = (center, n['color'])

# Click (34, 13) - bottom red block
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 34, 'col': 13}])

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
small_blocks2 = [n for n in seg2['nodes'] if n['pixels'] == 4 and n['color'] not in ('N', 'B', 'g', 'G')]
positions_after = {}
changed = []
for n in small_blocks2:
    b = n['boundary']
    center = (sum(p[0] for p in b)//4, sum(p[1] for p in b)//4)
    h = n['hash'][:8]
    positions_after[h] = (center, n['color'])
    if h in positions_before and positions_before[h][0] != center:
        changed.append((h, positions_before[h][0], center, n['color']))

print(f"Blocks changed by (34,13): {len(changed)}")
for h, before, after, color in changed:
    print(f"  {color} hash={h}: {before} -> {after}")

# Check strip
green_nodes = [n for n in seg2['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N' and n['pixels'] > 10]
for n in green_nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    print(f"Green strip: pixels={n['pixels']}, rows {min(p[0] for p in b)}-{max(p[0] for p in b)}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": "VERIFIED: Each red block rotates a different ring. (17,20)->top ring, (25,13)->middle ring. Testing (34,13)->bottom ring. Strip shrinks 1px per any rotation.", "object_taxonomy": "Red blocks=ring-specific triggers, Green strip=progress bar, colored blocks=3 independent rings", "hud_map": "Left green strip shrinks 1px/rotation", "win_pattern": "Likely: align all 3 rings to target config", "level_log": "Level 1: 8 rotations. Level 2: discovered 3 independent rings.", "cross_level_notes": "Level 2 has 3 independent rotation rings, each controlled by a different red block"}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "3 rings: top (row~17), middle (row~26), bottom (row~35). Each has its own red trigger.", "goal_model": "Rotate each ring independently to match target pattern", "recent_findings": ["(25,13) only rotates middle ring, not top ring", "Each red block controls a different ring"], "open_questions": ["Which ring does (34,13) control?", "What is the target configuration for each ring?"], "current_plan": "Test (34,13) to confirm it controls bottom ring, then figure out target config", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
